title: nomatch
text: nomatch package uses G-computation style estimation compute marginal cumulative incidence observational cohort studies binary exposure time--event outcome. approach aligns target trial emulation framework (Hernán Robins 2016) provides alternative matching-based methods, can inefficient. Estimation involves fitting two exposure-specific Cox models adjust baseline confounders. Predictions models used estimate marginal cumulative incidence interventions exposure exposure. Effect measures risk differences, risk ratios, relative risk reduction also provided.

title: nomatch
text: use nomatch estimate effectiveness binary exposure observational cohort study. package can loaded follows: illustrate use package using simple simulated dataset, simdata, included package. exposition, assume dataset represents data observational vaccine study. practice, data disease settings can used well. first rows simdata can viewed follows: data contains one row per individual (ID) set individual baseline covariates (x1, x2). Information individual’s exposure encompassed 1) binary vaccination status V values 1 = vaccinated, 0 = unvaccinated, 2) time receiving vaccination D_obs, numeric value vaccinated individuals NA unvaccinated individuals. data also includes right-censored survival outcome(Y, event) Y represents follow-time outcome (e.g. infection death), event indicates whether individual experienced event values 1 = event, 0 = censored.

title: nomatch
text: can invoke nomatch function compute marginal cumulative incidences. main types arguments : Data arguments- data, outcome_time, outcome_status, exposure, exposure_time, covariates: Specify dataset variable names describing outcome, exposure, confounders. Estimation arguments control cumulative incidences computed: immune_lag excludes events occuring immune_lag time units exposure. common vaccine studies, immunity takes time develop. example set immune_lag = 14 days, although can set 0 relevant. timepoints evaluate cumulative incidence. compute cumulative incidence 30, 60, 90, …, 180 days vaccination. Bootstrap argument - boot_repscontrols many bootstrap replicates used construct confidence intervals p-values. use small number speed, boot_reps = 1000 recommended publication-quality results. default, Wald-type bootstrap confidence intervals returned. cumulative incidence estimates (cuminc_0 unexposed cuminc_1 exposed) ) effectiveness measures stored within $estimates component fitted object. can thus preview relative risk reduction estimates (.e. vaccine effectiveness) using confounders adjusted , resulting cumulative incidence/effect estimates can interpreted cumulative incidences effect observed clinical trial participants 1) similar observed exposed 2) enroll study distribution enrollments times matches distribution observed exposure times.

title: nomatch
text: Internally, nomatch() fits two Cox models — one unexposed, one exposed. unexposed model, time endpoint measured study start (chosen time origin). model includes individuals, exposed individuals censored time exposure. model adjusts fo baseline covariates. exposed model, time endpoint measured time exposure. model includes exposed individuals remained event-free immune_lag days exposure. model adjusts baseline covariates flexibly accounts exposure occurred (default, using natural cubic spline 4 degrees freedom). Cox models used predict time- covariate-specific cumulative incidences marginalized. default, predictions marginalized distribution exposure times covariates exposed. summary estimation approach fitted models can obtained weights used marginalization returned $weights component fitted object.

title: nomatch
text: plot shows pointwise confidence intervals, usual 95% CIs computed independently timepoint. inference entire curve desired, simultaneous confidence intervals used instead. can added using add_simultaneous_ci(). function updates existing fit simultaneous confidence intervals, provided bootstrap samples retained (keep_boot_samples = TRUE, default). updated fit saved new object. simultaneous confidence intervals now stored fitted object. plot simultaneous confidence intervals, use plot() argument ci_type = "simul". fitted object must already contain simultaneous confidence intervals; otherwise, call return error.  simultaneous confidence intervals indicate 95% bands, constructed across repeated samples, contain entire true curve.
code: #Compute simultaneous CI fit_with_simul <- add_simultaneous_ci(fit, seed = 1234) fit_with_simul #>  #>  Risk Ratio Estimates  #> ==================================================  #> Call: nomatch(data = simdata, outcome_time = "Y", outcome_status = "event",  #>     exposure = "V", exposure_time = "D_obs", covariates = c("x1",  #>         "x2"), immune_lag = 14, timepoints = seq(30, 180, by = 30),  #>     boot_reps = 10)  #>  #> Result: #>   Timepoint Estimate 95% Wald CI: Lower 95% Wald CI: Upper Wald p-value #> 1        30    0.534              0.305              0.936     1.03e-01 #> 2        60    0.605              0.514              0.712     1.92e-06 #> 3        90    0.603              0.535              0.680     8.02e-11 #> 4       120    0.661              0.610              0.716     0.00e+00 #> 5       150    0.731              0.643              0.830     3.67e-05 #> 6       180    0.828              0.710              0.965     2.81e-02 #>   95% Simul CI: Lower 95% Simul CI: Upper #> 1               0.261               1.093 #> 2               0.491               0.744 #> 3               0.518               0.703 #> 4               0.597               0.732 #> 5               0.620               0.860 #> 6               0.681               1.007 #>  #> Use summary() for more details #> Use plot() to visualize results #Plot simultaneous confidence bands  plot(fit_with_simul , effect = "relative_risk_reduction", ci_type = "simul")

text: Users may also wish create plots. function estimates_to_df() takes estimates component fitted object reformats long dataset suitable plotting. example, can create plot overlays cumulative incidence estimates two exposure types follows:
code: plot_data <- estimates_to_df(fit)  head(as_tibble(plot_data)) #> # A tibble: 6 × 8 #>      t0 term             estimate wald_lower wald_upper wald_se wald_pval wald_n #>   <dbl> <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1    30 cuminc_0          0.0116     0.0109     0.0125  0.0359   NA           10 #> 2    30 cuminc_1          0.00622    0.00369    0.0105  0.268    NA           10 #> 3    30 risk_difference  -0.00542   -0.00904   -0.00180 0.00185   0.00336     10 #> 4    30 risk_ratio        0.534      0.305      0.936   0.286     0.103       10 #> 5    30 relative_risk_r…  0.466      0.0642     0.695   0.286     0.103       10 #> 6    60 cuminc_0          0.0379     0.0357     0.0402  0.0315   NA           10 plot_data |>    filter(term %in% c("cuminc_0", "cuminc_1")) |>    ggplot(aes(x = t0, y = estimate, color = term, fill = term)) +    geom_line() +    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = wald_lower, ymax = wald_upper), alpha = 0.2, linewidth = 0) +    theme_bw() +   scale_color_discrete(breaks = c("cuminc_0", "cuminc_1"), labels = c("Unvaccinated", "Vaccinated"))+   scale_fill_discrete(breaks = c("cuminc_0", "cuminc_1"), labels = c("Unvaccinated", "Vaccinated"))+    labs(x = "Time since vaccination",        y = "Estimate",        title = "Cumulative Incidence Curves",         color = "Exposure Group",        fill = "Exposure Group")

title: nomatch
text: estimation approach generally expected produce similar point estimates rolling-cohort matching analysis exposed individuals matched unexposed individuals time exposure. However, approach may offer greater precision, can particularly beneficial smaller samples endpoints rare. comparison, provide simple implementation rolling-cohort matching analysis using 1:1 exact matching Kaplan–Meier estimation compute marginal cumulative incidences. matching functions intentionally limited scope may slow large datasets.
code: # ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ # 3. Compare results with matching estimator  matched_cohort <- match_rolling_cohort(data = simdata,                                        outcome_time = "Y",                                        exposure = "V",                                        exposure_time = "D_obs",                                         matching_vars = c("x1", "x2"),                                        id_name = "ID",                                        seed = 5678)  matched_data <- matched_cohort[[1]]   fit_matching <-matching(matched_data = matched_data,                         outcome_time = "Y",                         outcome_status = "event",                         exposure = "V",                         exposure_time = "D_obs",                          immune_lag = 14,                         timepoints = seq(30, 180, by = 30),                         boot_reps = 10)  #> Bootstrapping 10 samples... #> Bootstrap completed in 1.47 secs  fit_matching #>  #>  Risk Ratio Estimates  #> ==================================================  #> Call: matching(matched_data = matched_data, outcome_time = "Y", outcome_status = "event",  #>     exposure = "V", exposure_time = "D_obs", immune_lag = 14,  #>     timepoints = seq(30, 180, by = 30), boot_reps = 10)  #>  #> Result: #>   Timepoint Estimate 95% Wald CI: Lower 95% Wald CI: Upper Wald p-value #> 1        30    0.497              0.316              0.783     0.029890 #> 2        60    0.517              0.397              0.672     0.000319 #> 3        90    0.611              0.488              0.766     0.000709 #> 4       120    0.671              0.561              0.804     0.000361 #> 5       150    0.710              0.606              0.832     0.000344 #> 6       180    0.775              0.622              0.966     0.045460 #>  #> Use summary() for more details #> Use plot() to visualize results  # Plot matching vs proposed estimator - nomatch tends to have similar point estimates but narrower # confidence intervals  comparison  <- rbind(estimates_to_df(fit) |> mutate(method = "nomatch (G-computation)"),                        estimates_to_df(fit_matching)  |> mutate(method = "matching"))  comparison |>    filter(term %in% c("cuminc_0", "cuminc_1", "relative_risk_reduction")) |>    mutate(term_label = factor(term,                               c("cuminc_0", "cuminc_1", "relative_risk_reduction"),                              c("Cumulative Incidence:\n Unvaccinated",                                "Cumulative Incidence:\n Vaccinated",                                "Relative Risk Reduction"))) |>    ggplot(aes(x = t0, y = estimate, color = method, fill = method)) +    geom_line() +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = wald_lower, ymax = wald_upper), alpha = 0.2, linewidth = 0.1) +    facet_wrap(~term_label, scales = "free") +    ggh4x::facetted_pos_scales(     y = list(       ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(),                                   limits = c(0, 0.11)),       ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(),                                   limits = c(0, 0.11)),       ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(),                                   limits = c(0, 0.8))     )) +    theme_bw() +   theme(strip.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = "white", color = "white"),          strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 11, colour = "black")) +    labs(x = "Time since vaccination",         y = "Estimate",         color = "Method",        fill = "Method")

title: Add simultaneous confidence intervals to nomatchfit object — add_simultaneous_ci
text: object object class nomatchfit created nomatch() matching(). Must contain evaluations least 2 timepoints (length(object$timepoints)  > = 2), contain one bootstrap replicate (boot_reps > 1) contain non-null bootstrap samples (keep_boot_samples = TRUE fitting). seed Integer seed  ensure reproducible critical values simultaneous confidence intervals (relied random number generation). Default NULL (seed set).

Cumulative incidence matched analysis dataset estimated using Kaplan Meier stratified exposure group. Confidence intervals. Wald percentile confidence intervals constructed cumulative incidence effectiveness parameters timepoint. what: Compute conditional cumulative incidences from fitted Cox models — predict_from_model_0
title: Compute conditional cumulative incidences from fitted Cox models — predict_from_model_0
text: functions compute conditional time- covariate-specific cumulative incidence row newdata. predict_from_model_0() computes \(\psi_0(t_0; d, x) = 1 - S_0(d+t_0 \mid x)/S_0(d+\tau \mid x)\) calling predict(fit_0, newdata, type = "survival") times d + t0 d + tau. predict_from_model_1() computes \(\psi_1(t_0; d, x) = 1 - S_1(t_0 \mid d, x)/S_1(\tau \mid d, x)\) calling predict(fit_1, newdata, type = "survival")  times t0 tau.
